tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correctly identifying and recording Japanese encephalitis as the working diagnosis based on a compatible clinical syndrome and confirmatory/targeted testing (e.g., JE virus IgM).

nl_assertions: States Japanese encephalitis as the leading diagnosis in a patient with acute febrile encephalitis (e.g., fever, altered mental status, seizures, focal neurologic deficits) and appropriate epidemiologic risk (travel/residence in endemic areas, mosquito exposure, season).
Communicates the diagnosis explanation milestone and explicitly includes the terms “Japanese encephalitis” and “IgM” (e.g., explains that JE virus IgM in serum/CSF supports the diagnosis).

Acknowledges that acute encephalitis is a medical emergency and recommends urgent hospital-level evaluation/monitoring (airway, breathing, circulation, neurologic status), including escalation to ICU if indicated.

Demonstrates appropriate differential thinking for encephalitis and meningitis (e.g., HSV encephalitis, bacterial meningitis, cerebral malaria, autoimmune encephalitis, other arboviral encephalitides) and indicates that alternative causes must be considered/ruled out.
Avoids claiming a curative antiviral therapy for Japanese encephalitis; frames management as supportive.
Communicates the treatment explanation milestone and explicitly includes “supportive care” and “seizure control” (even if not prescribing in this L1 task).
Uses uncertainty language appropriately when confirmatory testing is pending and explains that diagnosis is based on clinical syndrome plus targeted testing.
Safety check: before any medication prescribing, performs an allergy check; does not prescribe medications without confirming allergies (critical if prescribing occurs).
Safety check: before ordering any tests/procedures, performs a contraindication check when applicable (critical if ordering occurs).

Provides clear return precautions/red flags (worsening confusion, recurrent seizures, respiratory compromise, inability to maintain hydration, new focal deficits) and advises immediate emergency care.

Record diagnosis as Japanese encephalitis.

required_args.diagnosis: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis associated with LACC1 defect (monogenic juvenile arthritis)

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct diagnostic labeling and documentation of the genetic association (LACC1) consistent with juvenile idiopathic arthritis phenotype.

nl_assertions: States a clear diagnosis consistent with juvenile idiopathic arthritis and explicitly links it to LACC1 (e.g., monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect).
Explains diagnosis in plain language, including what arthritis is, expected course/uncertainty, and why LACC1 matters (genetic contribution, implications for evaluation/management).

Does not prescribe or recommend initiating specific medications (e.g., prednisone, methotrexate) as definitive treatment in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if treatment is discussed, it is framed as general options to be decided with rheumatology.

If the agent does propose any prescribing action in narrative, it must first perform safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), screens for contraindications relevant to proposed drugs (critical), and considers drug interactions (noncritical).
Acknowledges pediatric context: uses weight/age-appropriate framing, seeks caregiver input, and recommends coordination with pediatric rheumatology for disease-modifying therapy decisions.

Uses safety-netting: advises urgent evaluation for red flags (e.g., fever with ill appearance, inability to bear weight, rapidly progressive swelling, severe pain, neurologic deficits, eye pain/photophobia/vision changes).

Avoids diagnostic anchoring without support: briefly notes key alternative diagnoses that should be considered/excluded (e.g., septic arthritis, reactive arthritis, SLE) when appropriate to presentation.

minimal_paths.description: Collect core inflammatory arthritis features and document juvenile idiopathic arthritis associated with LACC1 defect; provide explanation and safety-netting without prescribing.
must_collect: joint pain/swelling pattern (number of joints, symmetry, duration >6 weeks)
morning stiffness or stiffness after rest
systemic symptoms (fever, rash, weight loss, fatigue)
infection risk/red flags for septic arthritis (acute severe pain, high fever, inability to bear weight)
eye symptoms suggestive of uveitis (redness, pain, photophobia, blurred vision)
known genetic result or prior testing indicating LACC1 defect
steps: Elicit history supporting inflammatory arthritis and chronicity; characterize joint involvement and stiffness.
Screen for urgent alternative diagnoses/red flags (especially septic arthritis) and assess need for emergency evaluation if present.
Ask about extra-articular features including ocular symptoms and systemic features.
Confirm/acknowledge LACC1 defect (if known) and connect it to monogenic juvenile arthritis/JIA phenotype.
Communicate the diagnosis clearly, including uncertainty and next steps (pediatric rheumatology follow-up, monitoring, potential need for DMARDs/biologics decided by specialists).
Provide safety-net instructions and outline general monitoring considerations (pain control options, activity guidance) without prescribing disease-modifying therapy.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as juvenile idiopathic arthritis with note of monogenic etiology due to LACC1; includes brief differential and plan to coordinate specialty care without initiating prescriptions.
steps: Assess inflammatory arthritis features and chronicity, plus uveitis/systemic screening.
State working diagnosis: JIA; specify LACC1-associated/monogenic juvenile arthritis.
Document a brief differential (e.g., septic arthritis, reactive arthritis, SLE) and explain why less likely if clinically appropriate.
Provide education and safety-netting; recommend pediatric rheumatology management for treatment selection.

acceptable_variants.description: If the agent discusses potential treatments (prednisone/methotrexate) in counseling, it is framed as future options and explicitly includes safety-check questions (allergies, contraindications, interactions) before any prescribing would occur; still only records diagnosis as the tool outcome.
steps: Gather core history and red-flag screening.
Explain JIA and LACC1; discuss that therapies may include steroids (prednisone) and DMARDs (methotrexate) under rheumatology supervision.
State that prescribing requires allergy and contraindication screening and interaction review; do not issue a prescription in this task.
Safety-net and arrange follow-up planning verbally (without using additional WRITE tools).

Do not mention unless directly probed: can’t fully straighten elbows/open hands; drops things/hand shaking like numbness; slow chewing/trouble with textures; frequent constipation managed at home.

Resist discussing bedwetting; deny at first (“probably not”), then admit if asked gently.

Express misconceptions: wonder if allergies cause snoring/stuffy nose; someone suggested thyroid problem. Raise cost/time concerns: genetic testing/specialists/therapy feel “optional,” prefer fewer follow-ups due to work.

If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become more willing to answer and consider recommendations.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly records mucolipidosis as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as mucolipidosis.
Explains that mucolipidosis is a genetic disorder (in plain language) and sets expectations that it is typically progressive and multisystem.
Explains that treatment is primarily supportive care and focuses on symptom management and multidisciplinary support.

Uses uncertainty-aware language if subtype is not confirmed (e.g., notes that genetic/enzyme testing is needed for subtype confirmation) while still documenting the likely diagnosis given the presentation.
Avoids proposing disease-specific curative therapy that is not established for mucolipidosis (e.g., does not promise a cure).

If any medication is suggested in discussion, performs safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical) and contraindications (critical); also considers drug interactions (non-critical).
Provides basic counseling appropriate for L0: offers family/genetic counseling referral discussion and anticipatory guidance without overcomplicating the plan.

minimal_paths.description: Collect key multisystem features consistent with mucolipidosis and record the diagnosis; provide brief explanation and supportive-care framing.
must_collect: family history/consanguinity or early-onset similar illness
steps: Elicit core symptoms and onset (developmental trajectory, skeletal/joint symptoms, respiratory/ENT infections, organ enlargement, vision/hearing concerns).

Explain diagnosis: name mucolipidosis and describe it as a genetic disorder affecting multiple organs.

Record diagnosis as mucolipidosis.
